body:
{
  "title": "Jin Castillo — Founder & CEO, Keel Networks",
  "compiled_truth": "Jin Castillo is the founder and CEO of [Keel Networks](companies/keel-networks), a cybersecurity startup he launched in early 2026 after spending nearly a decade at various infrastructure and developer-tools companies. Before Keel, he held senior engineering and product roles at mid-stage startups focused on network observability and API gateways — the kind of companies that never quite become household names but quietly power huge swaths of internet traffic. That background gave Jin an unusually deep understanding of where data integrity breaks down at scale, and it's the lens through which he built Keel's initial product thesis.

Castillo is known among peers for being a long-term thinker, sometimes frustratingly so. He's the type to table a promising feature because the architectural implications three years out don't sit right with him. People who've worked with him describe meetings where he'll sit quietly for ten minutes, sketching dependency graphs on a whiteboard, before offering a single decisive opinion. Analytical to a fault, maybe, but it's earned him credibility with technical co-founders and infrastructure buyers who distrust hype cycles.

Keel Networks under Jin's leadership has focused on deterministic authentication layers for enterprise environments — a niche that overlaps meaningfully with what [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor-codec) is building on the compression and transmission side. The two companies first crossed paths in mid-2027 at a working group on packet-level integrity, and since then Jin has been a recurring figure in Anchor's orbit. He's publicly stated that he sees single-pass data primitives as a foundational shift for cybersecurity, not just performance engineering, and has floated the idea of deeper integration between Keel's authentication stack and Anchor's pipeline.

Personally, Jin keeps a low profile. He doesn't do podcasts or Twitter threads. He's spoken at a handful of security-focused conferences — usually the kind with 200-person attendance and no livestream. His talks tend to be dense, slide-heavy, and well-regarded afterword in niche Slack channels. He lives in Portland, Oregon, is a distance runner, and is reportedly working on a long essay about the "myth of defense in depth" that he's been drafting for over a year.

At 38, Castillo is older than the median first-time founder in his cohort but younger than most cybersecurity CEOs with comparable technical depth. That positioning — experienced enough to be taken seriuosly, young enough to build for decades — is something he seems to lean into deliberately.",
  "timeline": "- **2026-01-18** | Jin Castillo incorporates [Keel Networks](companies/keel-networks) in Delaware; begins recruiting founding engineering team out of Portland.
- **2026-04-02** | Closes a $3.8M seed round led by a small infrastructure-focused fund; terms undisclosed.
- **2026-09-11** | Presents "Integrity Without Overhead" at PacSecWest, a regional security conference. Attendance ~180 people.
- **2027-02-07** | Keel ships its first enterprise pilot to a mid-size financial services firm; Jin personally leads the onboarding.
- **2027-06-19** | First documented interaction with [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor-codec) at a packet-level integrity working group hosted in San Francisco.
- **2027-08-30** | Jin and Anchor's infrastructure lead hold a follow-up call to discuss potential interoperability between Keel's auth layer and Anchor's single-pass pipeline.
- **2027-11-14** | Keel raises a $12M Series A; Jin notes in internal comms that the round gives them runway through mid-2029.
- **2028-01-22** | Speaks on a private panel about deterministic security guarantees, references Anchor Codec's approach as "the most interesting thing happening adjacent to our space."
- **2028-05-03** | Keel and Anchor begin a joint proof-of-concept engagement with an unnamed logistics company.
- **2028-07-16** | Jin publishes the first section of his long-delayed essay on defense-in-depth critique; circulates widely in infrastructure security circles."
}
